questions.question: The 19th Amendment to the US Constitution, ratified in 1920, guaranteed women the right to vote. How long had women's suffrage activists been fighting for this right before it passed?
options: 5 years
10 years
25 years
72 years
questions.explanation: The women's suffrage movement is often traced to the Seneca Falls Convention of 1848, where Elizabeth Cady Stanton drafted the Declaration of Sentiments demanding equal rights including the vote. The 72-year struggle involved generations of activists including Susan B. Anthony, Sojourner Truth, and Ida B. Wells. Some of the women who began the fight in 1848 did not live to see its success in 1920.
questions.memory_hook: The suffrage movement fought for 72 years from Seneca Falls (1848) to the 19th Amendment (1920). Many who started the fight in 1848 didn't live to see victory in 1920.

questions.question: The RMS Titanic sank on April 15, 1912, after striking an iceberg. How many people died out of approximately 2,224 aboard?
questions.explanation: Approximately 1,500 of the 2,224 people aboard the Titanic died — about 68% — making it one of the deadliest peacetime maritime disasters. The ship carried enough lifeboats for only about 1,178 people and many launched only partially full. The disparity in survival rates was stark: about 60% of first-class passengers survived vs. only 24% of third-class passengers. The wreck was discovered on the ocean floor in 1985 by Robert Ballard.
questions.memory_hook: 1,500 of 2,224 died — partly because lifeboats were only 33% full and partly because wealthy first-class passengers survived at 2.5x the rate of third-class. Class determined survival more than proximity to the iceberg.

questions.question: The First Opium War (1839-42) between Britain and China began because Britain was illegally importing what into China?
options: Weapons to Chinese rebels
Opium from British India to pay for Chinese goods
Christian missionaries
Cotton textiles
questions.answer: Opium from British India to pay for Chinese goods
questions.explanation: Britain was running a massive trade deficit buying Chinese goods (tea, silk, porcelain) but had little China wanted. The East India Company solved this by growing opium in India and selling it to Chinese merchants. When China's commissioner Lin Zexu confiscated and destroyed 20,000 chests of opium in 1839, Britain declared war. Britain won, China ceded Hong Kong, and opium imports continued. By 1900, an estimated 27 million Chinese were addicted.
questions.memory_hook: Britain created 27 million opium addicts in China to balance its trade deficit buying Chinese tea. When China fought back, Britain went to war to protect the right to sell drugs.

questions.question: Lance Armstrong won seven consecutive Tour de France titles (1999-2005). What happened to these titles?
options: He voluntarily gave back two titles after admitting use of performance-enhancing drugs
All seven were stripped by the UCI after evidence of systematic doping was confirmed
He was allowed to keep them under an anti-doping exemption
He returned them only after losing a civil lawsuit
questions.answer: All seven were stripped by the UCI after evidence of systematic doping was confirmed
questions.explanation: Lance Armstrong's entire cycling legacy was destroyed in 2012 when the US Anti-Doping Agency released a 1,000-page report documenting systematic, team-wide doping during all seven Tour victories. Armstrong initially denied everything vigorously, suing accusers, before finally admitting it in a 2013 interview with Oprah Winfrey. The UCI stripped all his titles, leaving him with no victories from August 1998 onward.
questions.memory_hook: Armstrong denied doping for years while suing people who told the truth. His 2013 Oprah confession came after a 1,000-page USADA report made denial impossible — all 7 titles were stripped.

questions.question: Secretariat won the Triple Crown in 1973, setting records that still stand in all three races. What are the three races that make up the Triple Crown?
options: Belmont Stakes, Preakness, and Breeders' Cup
Kentucky Derby, Preakness Stakes, and Belmont Stakes
Pimlico Stakes, Churchill Downs Classic, and Saratoga Cup
Kentucky Derby, Arlington Million, and Belmont Stakes
questions.answer: Kentucky Derby, Preakness Stakes, and Belmont Stakes
questions.explanation: The American Triple Crown consists of three horse races for 3-year-old thoroughbreds: the Kentucky Derby (Churchill Downs, Louisville, KY; 1.25 miles), the Preakness Stakes (Pimlico, Baltimore, MD; 1.1875 miles), and the Belmont Stakes (Belmont Park, NY; 1.5 miles). Secretariat won all three in 1973, setting times that still stand as records. His Belmont Stakes win by 31 lengths is widely considered the greatest performance in horse racing history.
questions.memory_hook: The Triple Crown is Kentucky Derby → Preakness → Belmont. Secretariat won all three in 1973 and his record times in all three still haven't been broken over 50 years later.

questions.question: Stranger Things (Netflix, 2016–present), created by the Duffer Brothers, became a global phenomenon and helped define Netflix's identity as a prestige platform. What cultural phenomenon did it trigger?
options: It launched the 'shared universe' model in television — its interconnected plot threads required viewers to watch a companion app and podcast to fully understand the story, pioneering transmedia storytelling for streaming

It ignited a massive nostalgia-driven cultural wave for 1980s pop culture — its precise recreation of 1983–1985 Hawkins, Indiana (Spielberg films, Dungeons & Dragons, synth music) made retro aesthetics commercially dominant for years

It was the first series commissioned directly by Netflix based on streaming data rather than creative pitching — its development was driven entirely by analytics showing high demand for supernatural teen drama
It pioneered the 'binge release' model that all streaming platforms now use — Netflix releasing all episodes simultaneously on launch weekend was invented specifically for Stranger Things Season 1
questions.answer: It ignited a massive nostalgia-driven cultural wave for 1980s pop culture — its precise recreation of 1983–1985 Hawkins, Indiana (Spielberg films, Dungeons & Dragons, synth music) made retro aesthetics commercially dominant for years
questions.explanation: Stranger Things was a love letter to 1980s Spielberg (E.T., Close Encounters) and Stephen King, set in a fictional Indiana town with a monster (the Demogorgon) from a parallel dimension called the Upside Down. Its success triggered a decade-long 80s nostalgia wave in fashion, music, design, and entertainment. Season 4's 'Running Up That Hill' by Kate Bush (1985) re-entered charts worldwide after 37 years and reached No. 1 in the UK. Netflix reported Stranger Things Season 4 was viewed for 1.15 billion hours in its first 28 days — a streaming record.
questions.memory_hook: Stranger Things Season 4 sent Kate Bush's 1985 'Running Up That Hill' to No. 1 in the UK — 37 years after its original release. The show's Spielberg-soaked 1980s nostalgia made retro aesthetics dominant in fashion and culture for years.

questions.question: David Bowie's 1972 album 'The Rise and Fall of Ziggy Stardust and the Spiders from Mars' featured which fictional alien rock star persona?
options: Ziggy Stardust
Aladdin Sane
Major Tom
Thin White Duke
questions.explanation: Ziggy Stardust was Bowie's androgynous alien rock star alter ego — extravagant, androgynous, and otherworldly, with an orange mullet and outrageous costumes. The album concept follows Ziggy, Earth's last rock star, before the apocalypse. Bowie's persona shifts (Aladdin Sane, Major Tom, the Thin White Duke, Halloween Jack) are each distinct character creations. He is credited with creating the concept of the artist as a shape-shifting persona.
questions.memory_hook: Bowie's Ziggy Stardust (1972) introduced an alien androgynous rock star persona — and the concept of an artist as shapeshifter. He created multiple distinct personas throughout his career, each with a different look and sound.

questions.question: Fyodor Dostoevsky's 'Crime and Punishment' (1866) follows a student who commits murder and then struggles with guilt. In which Russian city is it set?
options: Moscow
St. Petersburg
Kiev
Minsk
questions.explanation: Crime and Punishment is set in St. Petersburg in the sweltering summer of the 1860s. The protagonist, Raskolnikov, murders a pawnbroker believing extraordinary people are above moral law — then suffers psychological torment. Dostoevsky wrote the novel while deeply in debt, dictating it to a stenographer (Anna Snitkina, who became his wife) to meet a publishing deadline. The novel is considered a foundational work of psychological literature.
questions.memory_hook: Dostoevsky wrote Crime and Punishment under debt so crushing he had to dictate it to meet a deadline — and his stenographer became his wife. Raskolnikov's guilt is one of literature's great psychological portraits.

questions.question: The tundra biome, characterized by permafrost and extremely low temperatures, is found primarily in which geographic regions?
options: Tropical high mountains
Arctic regions and alpine zones above the treeline
Temperate grasslands
Coastal desert regions
questions.answer: Arctic regions and alpine zones above the treeline
questions.explanation: Tundra covers about 20% of Earth's land surface in two main forms: Arctic tundra (circling the Arctic Ocean across Alaska, Canada, Russia, Scandinavia, and Greenland) and alpine tundra (above the tree line on high mountains worldwide). The key characteristic is permafrost — permanently frozen soil that prevents trees from rooting. Tundra stores enormous amounts of carbon in frozen organic matter; melting permafrost due to climate change could release vast quantities of methane.
questions.memory_hook: Tundra covers 20% of Earth's land and stores massive carbon in its permafrost. As the Arctic warms, thawing permafrost releases methane — a feedback loop that accelerates warming further.

questions.question: Hawaii's islands were formed by volcanic activity associated with a 'hotspot' — a plume of magma rising through the Pacific Plate. In which direction has the Pacific Plate been moving, as shown by the chain of islands?
options: South to north
West to east
Northwest (the older islands are northwest of the newer ones)
East to west
questions.answer: Northwest (the older islands are northwest of the newer ones)
questions.explanation: The Hawaiian-Emperor seamount chain shows the Pacific Plate has been moving northwest over the stationary hotspot for about 70 million years. The Big Island of Hawaii is the youngest and still actively volcanic; the islands to the northwest are older and more eroded; the seamounts to the northwest (Emperor chain) are ancient sunken islands. Kauai (the northwesternmost main island) is about 5 million years old; the Big Island's Mauna Loa is still active.
questions.memory_hook: The Hawaiian island chain is a record of the Pacific Plate moving northwest over a hotspot. The Big Island is youngest; Kauai is oldest among the main islands. The chain keeps extending southeast — a new island (Lōʻihi) is forming underwater.

questions.question: Sound travels as waves of compressed and rarefied air (or other medium). What determines the pitch (frequency) of a sound?
options: The amplitude (loudness) of the wave
The number of wave cycles per second (frequency, measured in Hertz)
The distance the sound must travel
The material through which it travels
questions.answer: The number of wave cycles per second (frequency, measured in Hertz)
questions.explanation: Pitch is determined by frequency: more wave cycles per second = higher pitch (higher frequency). The human ear can detect sounds from about 20 Hz (low rumble) to 20,000 Hz (high squeal). Middle C on a piano is 261.6 Hz. Dogs hear up to ~65,000 Hz; bats navigate by ultrasound (beyond 100,000 Hz). Amplitude determines loudness, not pitch. Sound travels ~343 m/s in air — much slower than light (3×10⁸ m/s).
questions.memory_hook: Pitch = frequency (cycles per second). The human range is 20 to 20,000 Hz. Bats hear 100,000+ Hz to echolocate in the dark — we can't hear the sounds they use to navigate.

questions.question: The nitrogen cycle is essential to all life. What role do nitrogen-fixing bacteria play that makes them critically important?
options: They convert atmospheric nitrogen (N₂) into ammonia/nitrates that plants can absorb
They break down dead organisms into nitrogen gas
They transport nitrogen through the food chain
They remove excess nitrogen from over-fertilized soils
questions.answer: They convert atmospheric nitrogen (N₂) into ammonia/nitrates that plants can absorb
questions.explanation: About 78% of Earth's atmosphere is nitrogen gas (N₂), but plants can't use it directly. Nitrogen-fixing bacteria (including Rhizobium in root nodules of legumes) convert N₂ into ammonia (NH₃) and then nitrates (NO₃⁻) that plants can absorb. Without nitrogen fixation, plants couldn't make proteins and DNA — life would be impossible. The Haber-Bosch process (industrial nitrogen fixation) now produces synthetic fertilizers that feed roughly half of humanity.
questions.memory_hook: 80% of the air is nitrogen, but plants can't use it — they depend on bacteria to convert it into usable forms. Haber-Bosch industrial nitrogen fixation now feeds half of humanity with synthetic fertilizers.
